title: IELTS Reading: Why Most Advice Is Wrong
subtitle: Based on 10+ years working with thousands of Band 7, 8 & 9 students

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.liz_intro: Welcome to the Reading guide. Most advice you've heard about IELTS Reading is wrong — and following it is what's keeping your score stuck. Let's fix that.
lessons.liz_outro: Now we'll expose the three biggest myths holding your reading score back.

slides.title: IELTS Reading Crash Course
slides.subtitle: Why Most Advice Is Wrong (And What Actually Works)

cards.title: Strategy Per Question Type
cards.body: A specific method for matching headings, true/false/not given, and every other type — not one generic skim-scan approach.

cards.title: Wired Into Practice
cards.body: Every strategy connects directly to the Reading Question Bank, so you apply what you learn the same session.

cards.title: Mistakes Get Tracked
cards.body: Wrong answers feed Mistake Analysis so the same trap doesn't catch you twice.

cards.title: Why Common Advice Fails
cards.body: Discover the myths that actually make your reading score *worse*, not better.

cards.title: The Real Difference
cards.body: Learn what truly separates Band 6 students from those achieving Band 7, 8, and 9.

cards.title: Proven Strategy
cards.body: Walk away with one complete strategy you can apply to your very next practice test.

slides.title: Let's expose the myths holding you back from your target score.

lessons.title: Myth #1 — Just Practise More
lessons.liz_intro: The most popular IELTS advice is also the most dangerous. Practising without correction doesn't make you better — it makes you worse. Let's see why with the story of two learners.
lessons.liz_outro: If practice alone worked, every learner with hours of test attempts would have Band 9. They don't. The next myth explains why even structured practice can fail you.

slides.title: Just Practise More!
slides.intro: This is the most dangerous advice in IELTS preparation. Here's why it's completely wrong.

columns.label: Johnny
items: Five years driving on the farm

columns.label: Emma ✓
items: Passed on her very first attempt with flying colours.

columns.label: Johnny ✗
items: Failed five times despite all his "experience" and practice.

slides.title: Practise Without Correction = Reinforcing Bad Habits

items.title: Every time you practise incorrectly, you're actually making it harder to improve. Your brain is learning the wrong patterns, and they become more difficult to unlearn with each repetition.

lessons.title: Myth #2 — It's Just Time Management
lessons.liz_intro: If you think you just need to read faster, you're treating the symptom — not the disease. The next analogy will show you what's really going on.
lessons.liz_outro: Speed is a *result* of skill, not a substitute for it. Once we've buried Myth #2, the third — and biggest — myth is next.

items.title: If you believe this, you're focusing on the wrong problem entirely.

slides.title: The Wrong Diagnosis
slides.intro: Do the runners behind Usain Bolt have a *time management* problem... or a *running* problem?

slides.title: You Don't Have a TIME Problem — You Have a SKILLS Problem

blocks.text: When you develop the right reading skills, speed follows naturally. Trying to read faster without improving your skills is like trying to drive faster without learning how to control the car.

lessons.title: Myth #3 — Just Skim and Scan
lessons.liz_intro: Every IELTS teacher tells you to skim and scan. They're not wrong — they're just incomplete. The missing third skill is what gets you from Band 6 to Band 7+.
lessons.liz_outro: Now you know what *doesn't* work and what most teachers miss. Next, we'll build the system that *actually* gets results.

slides.title: Just Skim and Scan!
slides.intro: This advice is incomplete. It's not wrong, but it's only part of the story — and the missing piece is costing you marks.

columns.label: Skimming — what it actually looks like
items: Read the **first sentence** of each paragraph (this is usually the topic sentence — the main idea)
Read the **last sentence** if the paragraph is long
Look at any **headings, bold words, or numbers**
Goal: in 60 seconds, know *what each paragraph is about* — not the details

columns.label: Scanning — what it actually looks like
items: **Hold one keyword in your head** (e.g. *'pollution'* from the question)
Sweep your eyes down the page hunting for that word **OR a synonym** (*'pollution'* could appear as *'contamination'*, *'damage to the environment'*)
When you spot it, *stop* — that's where the answer lives
Goal: locate the right paragraph in under 30 seconds, then close-read it

slides.title: Skimming
slides.subtitle: Task: Find the McDonald's in Times Square

blocks.text: From this height, you can see the general area — but can you spot the exact location? This is what skimming gives you: the **big picture**.
You've found the location now. You can see Times Square clearly. But there are **multiple McDonald's locations** here. Which one is correct?
At street level, you need to read signs carefully, check addresses, and identify the exact location. This is **close reading** — the skill that actually gets you the answer.

slides.title: Most Teachers Only Teach Skim and Scan
slides.subtitle: Close Reading Is the Secret

items.title: This is the missing skill that separates Band 6 students from Band 7+ achievers. Without it, you're like someone searching Times Square from the air — you'll never find the precise answer.

lessons.title: What Actually Works
lessons.liz_intro: Now we shift from what's broken to what's proven. This is the foundation Band 7+ students build on — and it starts in a place most learners never look.
lessons.liz_outro: The system is clear. The next step is seeing it applied to a real question type — Matching Headings.

items.title: Now let's explore the proven system that creates real results.

slides.title: The Five-Level Pyramid
slides.intro: This pyramid represents the true foundation of IELTS Reading success. The wider the base, the more critical it is to your score. Most students make a fatal mistake with this pyramid.

steps.title: Tips
steps.body: Quick fixes, shortcuts, and last-minute advice.

steps.title: Vocabulary
steps.body: The fuel that powers everything else.

steps.title: Mindset
steps.body: The base everything is built on.

cards.title: 1. Most Students Start Here
cards.body: TIPS — Quick fixes, shortcuts, and last-minute advice.

cards.title: 2. You Should Start Here
cards.body: MINDSET — The foundation that supports everything else.

blocks.text: Building from tips down is like constructing a house from the roof. It might look impressive at first, but it will collapse.

cards.title: 01. Understand the Question
cards.body: Know exactly what's being asked before you start searching for answers.

cards.title: 02. Skimming
cards.body: Quickly grasp the overall meaning, structure, and main themes of the passage.

cards.title: 03. Scanning
cards.body: Locate keywords and identify the specific area containing your answer.

cards.title: 04. Close Reading
cards.body: Carefully analyse the relevant section to extract precise information.

cards.title: 05. Deciding the Correct Answer
cards.body: Use evidence from the text to confidently select or write your answer.

slides.title: Different Question Types Test Different Skills

blocks.text: Just as different road signs require different responses, each IELTS question type demands a specific combination of these five skills.
Would you take the same route to five completely different destinations?
Of course not. Yet this is exactly what most students do with IELTS Reading — they use one generic approach for every question type.

cards.title: Matching Headings
cards.body:
**Question:** Choose the correct heading for each paragraph from a list of options.

**Requires:** Understanding main ideas and paragraph structure

cards.title: True/False/Not Given
cards.body:
**Question:** Does the statement agree with the information in the passage?

**Requires:** Precise close reading and logical analysis

cards.title: Multiple Choice
cards.body:
**Question:** Choose the correct answer from several options.

**Requires:** Careful comparison and elimination of distractors

blocks.text: Each question type is testing different skills in different ways. Using the same approach for all of them guarantees you'll underperform. Now let's see exactly how this works in practice.

lessons.title: Worked Example — Matching Headings Strategy
lessons.liz_intro: Time to move from theory to action. We'll demo the Matching Headings question — the one most students lose marks on — first the wrong way, then the right way.
lessons.liz_outro: That's one question type cracked. The same logic applies to every other type — but vocabulary is what makes all of it possible, and that's what we'll cover next.

items.title: Watch how the wrong approach wastes time whilst the right strategy finds answers efficiently.

slides.title: Example Question
slides.subtitle: Task: Match each paragraph (A-E) with the correct heading (i-viii) from the list below.

blocks.body: This is one of the most challenging question types because there are always **extra headings** designed to confuse you. Many headings sound similar, and choosing incorrectly costs you marks.

blocks.text: Before we show you the proven method, let's look at the trap most students fall into.

slides.title: The Recipe for Failure
slides.intro: Each step here makes the next one worse — and you end up guessing at the end.

nodes.title: Read All Headings First
nodes.body: Become immediately confused by similar-sounding options

nodes.title: Read First 1-2 Sentences Only
nodes.body: Miss the main idea, which often comes later in the paragraph

nodes.title: Try to Match Immediately
nodes.body: Make rushed decisions based on incomplete information

nodes.title: OR Read Every Single Word
nodes.body: Waste precious time and lose focus on what matters

nodes.title: Run Out of Time
nodes.body: End up guessing on the final questions

blocks.text: This **six-step method** has helped thousands of students improve their scores. It works because it mirrors how skilled readers naturally process information.

slides.title: Steps 1 & 2 — Create Your Own Headings
slides.intro: This forces you to truly understand each paragraph instead of just scanning for keywords.

steps.title: DON'T look at the headings yet
steps.body: Avoid confusion and stay focused on understanding the text itself.

steps.title: Read each paragraph completely
steps.body: Then write YOUR heading in just 1-2 words summarising the main idea.

slides.title: Steps 3 & 4 — Match With Confidence
slides.intro: By this stage, you'll typically have **60-70% of answers** with complete confidence.

steps.title: NOW look at the actual headings
steps.body: Read each one carefully and underline the keywords in every heading.

steps.title: Compare and match obvious ones
steps.body: Match your headings with the actual options. The obvious matches will jump out immediately.

slides.title: Steps 5 & 6 — Solve the Difficult Ones
slides.intro: This systematic elimination ensures you're never just guessing — you're making evidence-based decisions.

steps.title: Write 2-3 possibilities
steps.body: For remaining paragraphs, note which headings could potentially match.

steps.title: Close read and decide
steps.body: Go back to the paragraph, read carefully, and use evidence to choose the correct answer.

items.title: Six steps. Every paragraph mapped. No guessing.

lessons.title: The Reading Test is NOT a Reading Test
lessons.liz_intro: Strategies only get you so far if you don't know the words. The IELTS Reading test is, more than anything, a vocabulary test. Let's fix vocabulary the smart way.
lessons.liz_outro: Strategies plus vocabulary is the system. The last step is knowing exactly what to do tomorrow morning.

slides.title: The Reading Test is NOT a Reading Test

items.title: It's a vocabulary test in disguise — and most students get this wrong.

slides.title: Synonyms and Paraphrasing in IELTS Reading
slides.intro: The same idea is almost never written the same way. Here's what that looks like in real IELTS Reading passages.

columns.label: Question Words
items: divided the day into two equal halves

columns.label: Words in the Text
items: split the day into two 12-hour periods

slides.title: Vocabulary Improvement Plan
slides.intro: A simple five-step routine you can start today and run for life.

steps.title: Read Daily
steps.body: Choose something you genuinely enjoy — articles, books, blogs. Just **10-15 minutes per day**.

steps.title: Note New Words
steps.body: Write down unfamiliar words as you encounter them, keeping a simple vocabulary journal.

steps.title: Guess From Context
steps.body: Before checking the dictionary, use surrounding words to guess the meaning.

steps.title: Verify Your Guess
steps.body: Check the definition and see how close you were — this builds your context skills.

steps.title: Review Regularly
steps.body: Revisit your vocabulary journal weekly to reinforce what you've learnt.

slides.title: Quick Check: Reading Principles
slides.intro: Five short questions to lock in what makes a strong reading test-taker before you move into the question-type strategies.

items.stem: Why does 'just practising more' often fail to raise reading scores?

options.text: Because more tests automatically mean lower attention.
Because practice without analysis just reinforces the same mistakes.
Because IELTS limits the number of practice tests.

items.explanation: Volume without correction = repeated mistakes at higher speed. Quality of review beats quantity of practice.

items.stem: Which of these is the BIGGEST time-management trap in IELTS Reading?

options.text: Spending too much time on one difficult question instead of moving on.

items.explanation: Time blown on one stuck question costs 2-3 easier questions later. Skip, mark, return at the end.

items.stem: The IELTS Reading test is, more than anything, a _______ test in disguise.
items.explanation: Strategies only work if you understand the words. Vocabulary growth is the highest-leverage long-term investment.

items.stem: What's the smart way to grow reading vocabulary?

options.text: Memorising long word lists by heart.
Reading material you genuinely enjoy and learning words in context.
Translating every unknown word as you read.

items.explanation: Words learned in context stick. Context-free flashcard memorisation is forgotten quickly and demoralising.

items.stem: Synonyms and paraphrasing mean the question almost never uses the _______ words as the passage.
items.explanation: IELTS deliberately rewords ideas. Train your eye to spot semantic equivalents, not surface-level word matches.

lessons.liz_outro: Reading is mastered through practice — and now you have the system, the strategy, and the place to apply both.

slides.title: Band 6 → Band 7 Isn't About Talent. It's About Having the Right System.

blocks.text: Every student we've helped from Band 6 to Band 7+ had one thing in common: they stopped relying on generic advice and started using **proven, question-specific strategies**. Talent is not required. The right system is.

slides.title: Your Next Steps
slides.intro: Three things to do this week — in this order.

steps.title: Open the Reading Question Bank
steps.body: Find a Matching Headings question and run the six-step method end-to-end. Time yourself, but don't rush the strategy.

steps.title: Master One Type Before Moving On
steps.body: Stay on Matching Headings until you're consistently confident, then move to True/False/Not Given — one type at a time.

steps.title: Bring Liz In When You Get Stuck
steps.body: Any time a question type confuses you, ask Liz to walk you through the strategy live.
